_comment: Workstream-D TEAM-decomposition suite. LONG-FORM drafting tasks ONLY — team decomposition (planner -> N section workers -> assembler) can only help where there is enough length to split. The existing book-chapter/content suites are micro-rubrics (<=175 words) and would show no team benefit. Each task carries a `domain` (book-chapter|content) used for the ledger namespace team:<domain>:<mode>. To add the REAL TSB chapter material, drop a task here with the surrounding-chapter context in `prompt` and matching judge criteria (keep narrative_coherence weighted high — that is where stitched sections fail).

tasks.prompt: Write a complete chapter (1800-2400 words) of a literary novel. The chapter follows Mara, a harbour-town clockmaker, across a single day in which a stranger commissions an impossible repair and she realises the town's tower clock has been running four minutes fast for a decade. Take the reader through: the morning workshop, the stranger's visit and commission, Mara's growing unease as she investigates the tower, and a closing beat that turns the discovery personal. Write it as ONE continuous chapter with scene breaks where needed. Concrete, in-scene, show-don't-tell; varied sentence rhythm; no AI tells, no summary narration, no meta-commentary. Return ONLY the prose.

deterministic.pattern: as an ai|i hope this|let me|in conclusion|here('|i)s (the|a) chapter|word count

criteria.name: narrative_coherence
criteria.guidance: Does it read as ONE coherent chapter with a continuous through-line, consistent voice/POV/tense, and smooth transitions — NOT disjointed blocks bolted together? Penalise seams, repeated setups, tonal shifts, or a section re-introducing what a prior section established.

criteria.name: scene_craft
criteria.guidance: Concrete scenes in medias res, sensory specifics, show-don't-tell. Penalise summary narration and abstract throat-clearing.

criteria.name: arc_and_momentum
criteria.guidance: Clear beginning/middle/end across the four beats with rising momentum and a closing turn that lands. Penalise a flat or truncated ending.

criteria.name: prose_quality
criteria.guidance: Sentence-level craft: varied rhythm, precise diction, no clichés or AI tells, no padding.

tasks.prompt: Write a complete, publication-ready long-form guide (1300-1800 words) titled 'How to Read a Company's Cash Flow Statement Without an Accounting Degree'. Audience: a small-business owner with no finance training. Cover: why the cash flow statement matters more than the P&L for survival; the three sections (operating, investing, financing) in plain English with a concrete worked mini-example each; the single most common misreading and how to avoid it; and a short do-this-now checklist. Use clear headings, concrete numbers in examples, and a confident, practical voice. No fluff, no AI tells. Return ONLY the article (markdown headings allowed).

deterministic.pattern: as an ai|i hope this helps|let me know|in conclusion,? (i|we) hope

criteria.name: structural_coherence
criteria.guidance: Reads as ONE coherent guide with logical flow and no redundancy across sections — NOT stitched blocks that repeat framing or contradict each other. Penalise seams, duplicated intros, or uneven depth between sections.

criteria.name: substantive_depth
criteria.guidance: Specific, correct, with concrete worked numeric examples for each of the three sections — not generic filler. Penalise vagueness and hand-waving.

criteria.name: completeness
criteria.guidance: Covers all required beats: why-it-matters, the three sections with examples, the common misreading, and the do-this-now checklist.

criteria.name: clarity_voice
criteria.guidance: Plain-English, confident practical voice pitched to a non-finance owner; clear headings; no jargon left unexplained, no padding or AI tells.

tasks._source: TSB issue 331da935 / comment 8aeed39f (THIAAAA-59 AI-draft quality pilot). Real published Book 1, Wickbarrow cozy mystery. Ch1+Ch3 are the bookends; ground truth Ch2 held in `groundTruth` (NOT in prompt) for the workstream-C missing-chapter diff test.
tasks.prompt:
You are drafting the MIDDLE chapter of a published cozy-mystery novel. You are given the full published text of the chapter BEFORE it and the chapter AFTER it, plus the story context. Write Chapter 2 so it bridges them seamlessly — honor every setup planted in Chapter 1, land the beats the context requires, and lead cleanly into Chapter 3. Match the established voice, POV, and tense exactly. Do NOT summarise, do NOT add notes or headings beyond the chapter title. Return ONLY the chapter prose.

=== STORY CONTEXT ===
Status: done

Below is the Book 1 packet for the AI-draft quality pilot. I chose Chapters 1-3 because continuity carries tightly across all three: Hen's tea ritual and threat against Felix in Chapter 1, Hen's death and Iris's first-scene observations in Chapter 2, and the Society's decision to investigate plus the medicine-cabinet frame in Chapter 3.

## Book premise / logline
At Larkspur Hall, a retirement community on the edge of the Cotswolds village of Wickbarrow-under-Marsh, four older residents form an unofficial society of amateur sleuths after the imperious Henrietta Vance dies in what first looks like a quiet heart attack. The mystery is fair-play cozy: every turn comes from clues the reader sees, with warmth, dry humor, and one locked diarist narrator.

## Why these three chapters
- They are fully consecutive and hinge on one continuous chain of cause and effect.
- Chapter 1 plants the 9pm chamomile ritual, Hen's public threat, Effie's tea-bringing, and Felix's visible strain.
- Chapter 2 delivers the body, the wrongness of the glasses on the book, the tea cup, and Iris pocketing the paper scrap.
- Chapter 3 converts the death into an investigation and explicitly frames the medical-access question that powers the next act.

## Main characters + their state at this point
- Iris Penhaligon, 71: retired cryptic-crossword editor, widowed, diarist narrator. At this point she is observant, controlled, and not yet openly grieving on the page. She has just crossed from observer into participant by pocketing the paper scrap in Chapter 2.
- Maggie Okoye, 68: retired GP. At this point she is the practical medical eye, calm under pressure, and already the person Iris trusts most with the uncomfortable truth.
- Hugo Trembath, 74: retired criminal-defence barrister. At this point he is theatrical, sharp, and newly energized by the possibility that something improper is happening.
- Dennis Albright, 69: retired estate agent and gossip-network specialist. At this point he is the social radar of the group, quick to jump to a conclusion but useful because he notices movements and people.
- Henrietta "Hen" Vance, 76: alive in Chapter 1, dead in Chapter 2. At this point in the story she has just threatened action over Felix and signalled there is a second, connected matter.
- Effie Mathers, 26: Hen's caregiver. At this point she is distressed, young, and clearly knows more about Hen's routines than anyone else.
- Felix Ardent, 58: Hall manager. At this point he looks pressured and defensive; the reader is meant to suspect him early.

## Middle chapter beat-sheet to reproduce faithfully
The removed middle chapter is Chapter 2, "The Body." It must do all of the following:
- Open immediately with Maggie knocking at Iris's door at 8:14am and urgently bringing her to Hen's flat.
- Put Effie in the corridor, distressed, having found Hen with the breakfast tray.
- Show Hen dead in bed in a room that appears almost too orderly.
- Emphasize the bedside tableau: the three-quarters-drunk cup of chamomile, untouched digestive, book on the bedside table, and reading glasses folded on top of the book.
- Let Iris notice that the glasses-on-book placement is wrong for Hen's habits.
- Let Iris notice and secretly pocket the torn corner of paper from the bedside table before the police can interpret it.
- Establish Maggie's medical professionalism and early caution around the cause of death.
- Move Effie out of the room into the Sun Room with Bert.
- Introduce DS Khatri at the end as the police presence who will matter going forward.
- End with Iris privately recording what she did wrong: straightening the cup, taking the paper, and not ringing the bell.

## POV, tense, and style guide
- POV: Iris diary entry, first person.
- Tense: past tense.
- Voice: dry, observant, exact about objects and habits; emotionally controlled rather than melodramatic.
- Paragraphing: short to medium paragraphs, with dateline breaks and clean forward motion.
- Mystery discipline: fair-play. The clues in the room must be plainly visible to the reader.
- Tone: cozy, humane, non-graphic. No thriller pulse, no on-page violence, no sentimental overreach.
- Signature habits: Iris notices hands, objects, books, how things are placed, and phrases doing more work than they should.

## Extra context a ghostwriter would need
- The tea ritual is not decorative. Effie's 9pm chamomile for Hen is a continuity lock from Chapter 1 into the cause-of-death puzzle.
- Hen's public threat in Chapter 1 creates immediate suspicion around Felix, but the novel is designed so that the obvious suspect is not enough.
- Iris does not openly discuss her own body or grief; even in shock she routes emotion through observation.
- Maggie is the only character who can credibly name the medical-access implications at this stage.
- Bert is already positioned as a background witness figure, even before his later importance becomes clear.
- Effie's surname is Mathers. Keep that exact continuity lock.

=== CHAPTER BEFORE (Chapter 1, published — full text) ===
## Bookend Chapter A (published text)

# Chapter 1 — The Tea Ritual

::: dateline
Wednesday — afternoon.
:::

The Society meets Wednesdays in the Library Room, between three and half-past four, with the fire if the porter has remembered, and a plate of digestives that Effie cuts in half because Hugo claims to be reducing. Reducing what, he never says. We pretend, all four of us, that we are not here for anything in particular. Then we sit down and discuss the things we are here for.

The room itself does a lot of the work. Panelled walls. The fireplace at the south end, where the brass dogs sit and pretend they have always sat. The long table that no one has ever managed to push into a useful position because the carpet has memory. The window faces the cedar that lost a limb in last March's storm, and beyond it the lawn that Eldermarsh keeps trimmed within an inch of its will to live. Some afternoons there is a bell from St Eadburh's drifting in over the wisteria, and on those afternoons Hugo looks at the ceiling and appears, briefly, to forgive his life.

Today the cedar was full of starlings and Hugo was not in a forgiving mood. He had spent the morning at the parish council. *Confirming what we already knew*, he said. *The boiler in the south corridor is original to the building, which is to say it is older than my grandfather and twice as obstinate.* Dennis, on the chair nearest the fire, told us the postcode of the company that had been asked to quote for the replacement, and a piece of gossip about the company's director that I shall not write down here. Maggie was pouring out. Lapsang, again. She has been on lapsang since her birthday in February, when Hugo gave her a tin of it and called it *medicinal*, which it isn't.

The porter brought the digestives at three minutes past, which is the porter's small punctuality joke at our expense. He has been delivering the digestives between five to three and the hour for years, and on the one Wednesday in 2022 when he genuinely had a reason to be late he was eleven minutes late and Hugo did not let him forget it for a fortnight. We have, in the porter, an audience who appreciates the regularity of our meetings without quite respecting them, and he, in us, has an audience who appreciates the regularity of his comings and goings without quite respecting them either. The arrangement is mutual and very English.

Bert Pollick went past the open door of the Library Room at five past three with a folded newspaper under one arm and the look of a man who would, if he were thirty years younger and inclined, have stopped in. He did not stop. He raised the newspaper at us, half an inch, in the way Bert raises things, and went on toward the conservatory.

Hugo was working through a story, in this period, about a barrister he had known in chambers in 1981 who had been compelled, by a clerical error, to argue a case in Birmingham in shoes one and a half sizes too small. The story did not change much in the retelling. Hugo had been telling it, with minor variations, since I had come to the hall, and the four of us had developed an unspoken agreement to allow him three minutes of it before Dennis interrupted. Dennis interrupted at two minutes and fifty seconds, on this Wednesday, with a piece of news about the Vicarage's roof slates that he had picked up at the post office. Hugo did not, on this Wednesday, complete the Birmingham story. He nodded, in the way one nods at a story one has been allowed to half-tell, and let Dennis have the floor. There was the small Hall arithmetic of these afternoons.

Then Hen arrived. She is not a member of the Society — she has declined, in writing, twice — but she is its weather. She arrives at our meetings the way rain arrives at a picnic: uninvited, conspicuous, and impossible to talk over. She is seventy-six years old and has not, in my hearing, fallen back on the phrase *I am not sure* since the autumn of 2019.

"I will not keep you long," she said, taking the chair Dennis vacated without comment. "I have warned him a number of times that I will not be patient indefinitely. I have given him a month to produce the accounts. I have given him a month to explain himself. He has —" and here she allowed herself a small economical smile "— produced excuses and explained nothing."

"Felix Ardent," said Hugo, "would explain water if you put a jug in front of him."

"He's bent," said Dennis, who likes a verdict before a trial.

"He may be bent," said Maggie. "We don't know yet what shape."

Hen accepted the cup the porter brought her. "I have written to the bishop's office for an appointment. Friday morning. I am not waiting for an audit. I am taking it where it will travel."

Hugo's eyebrows did the thing he stops them doing in court. "The bishop?"

"Felix is not the only matter at hand."

Hugo, for once, did not push the question. He looked at her, then at the fire, then at the digestives. Dennis leaned forward in the way he does when he hears a postcode he hadn't expected. Maggie said nothing. Maggie says nothing very well; it is one of the things she learned at her surgery and brought into retirement intact.

"Two matters," Hen said. "Connected, in their way. I shall sort one of them this week. The other has been waiting longer and may wait a little more." She drained the cup in three pulls. "The letter is posted, Hugo. *Posted* — not on my hall table."

"I would not have suggested otherwise."

"You were considering it." She rose. "I leave you, as ever, with the entertainment." The entertainment being us, presumably. "I will see you all at Effie's tea-bringing this evening. Nine, sharp. Chamomile only. The new girl in the kitchen put oat milk in it last Tuesday. I sent it back. We are not in California."

She left. The room re-arranged itself the way rooms do once Hen has left them.

"Nine sharp," said Dennis. "Punctual to within a digestive."

"She has been on chamomile for, what — twenty years?" said Maggie. "Effie brings it. Always Effie."

"Effie," said Hugo, "is the only person in this hall whom Hen is willing to be brought a cup of tea by. I find that quietly interesting."

"Effie is the only person in this hall whom Hen is willing to be in the same *room* with after nine in the evening," said Dennis. "Which I find quietly *more* interesting."

"Effie," said Maggie, "has been bringing that tea every night for two years and has, in my professional observation, never once chipped a saucer." She looked at me. "I include that for completeness."

I noted the smile she did not let through her mouth.

*Two matters*, Hen had said. *Connected, in their way.* Hen does not say *in their way* lightly. It is one of her honourable phrases, the kind she keeps for things she is not yet willing to put plainly. When she means a person, she names the person. When she means a sum, she names the sum. *In their way* belongs to her vocabulary of careful unsaying.

I am familiar with that vocabulary. I have my own.

The Society dispersed in its usual way. Hugo to the bar to read the *Times* in the chair he is reasonably sure has come to know him. Dennis to the post office on an errand he invented as he announced it. Maggie to the medication room — *a small re-stock, professional habit, don't wait* — and I to my flat on the second floor, where there are too many books and not enough shelf, and where the radiator clicks at unpredictable intervals like a small confession.

What I noticed on the way back:

Effie in the corridor with an empty tray, looking for the senior porter — an hour earlier than the tray usually goes anywhere. Hen at the south window, having a private conversation with the cedar tree. Felix Ardent in his office doorway, not seeing me, his face the colour of a man who has decided what he is going to do and is now arranging what he will say about it.

Felix's hands were doing something his face did not know about. The right one was opening and closing on the door frame. The left was holding a sheaf of papers I would have liked, in some other life, to read.

I went up. The lift had been broken on Tuesday and had not yet been mended; the lift has been mended four times this winter and is, increasingly, the kind of lift one does not mention to visitors. I took the staircase. I took it slowly, with my hand on the banister at the half landing, in the way I have taken stairs since the autumn. I do not, on the page, write about my left foot. I should rather not give it the dignity.

In the flat I put the kettle on. I opened the post — a circular, a bill from the optician, a postcard from a former colleague who has retired to Lewes and who continues to send me opinions on whether *the cryptic* has been allowed to become a *gentleman's diversion*. He is wrong, but he is consistent. I made a cup of breakfast tea. I let it stand. I let it go cold. I picked up the day's *Telegraph* crossword and did not, this afternoon, get further than nine across, which was a long anagram of *PARSIMONIOUS* and which I could not, this Wednesday, make my mind stop walking past.

::: dateline
Wednesday — late.
:::

I think Hen meant it this time. She does not usually mean them, which is the trick of her — a woman who threatens often enough that the room learns to round her down. But she did not bring up the bishop the way she brings up the boiler. She brought it up the way one moves a piece of furniture: with the new arrangement already in mind.

Hugo walked me to the corner of the Library Room and asked if I thought she would actually keep the appointment. *I think Hen keeps appointments*, I said. *She has never been one for the polite cancellation.* He nodded. Dennis was already heading off, having spotted, at the far end of the corridor, someone he could ask after. Maggie, who has known Hen longest, said nothing in particular and let her hands do the work — she folded her napkin into thirds and pressed it flat with a thumb, which is what Maggie does when she is thinking about something she has not yet given herself permission to say.

The trolley came down the corridor at five to nine. I heard the wheels. They run uneven, the offside catches every third turn. Effie's knock at Hen's door is a little three-tap-and-pause, polite without being meek, and Hen's *come* is, even from twenty yards, recognisably Hen's. The chamomile would have been steeped six minutes, the saucer the everyday white not the blue (Hen says the blue makes the tea taste of brides), the digestive on a side plate even though Hen never eats a digestive at nine in the evening — Effie brings the digestive because Hen complained the year before last that the saucer looked bare. Hen's complaints, once made, are permanent installations.

I have heard this trolley every Wednesday at five to nine since I moved into the flat above Hen's, which was the autumn of the year I shall not, in this notebook, mention; one starts counting time differently in March or one stops counting it at all, and I have not yet decided in which direction I am going. The trolley is, in any case, the small punctuation mark by which the second-floor corridor learns it is the end of the day. After the trolley, the porter does his last round; after the porter, the lights in the corridor go down a notch on the timer; after the lights, the building settles into its nightly low frequency of radiators and old plumbing. I have, on quiet Wednesdays, listened to all of it from this desk.

I sat at the writing desk for some time tonight without writing. The lamp is on the desk; the window above the desk faces the cedar from the other side; on the windowsill is a small photograph in a wooden frame and a glass with the last of a bouquet from the village shop, which is yellow and which I have not yet thrown away. I did not, this evening, write about the photograph.

Hen and I had had, last September, an exchange that I have not yet, in this notebook, set down. She had been at her best in it. I had been at my second best. She had said one specific thing, in the doorway of the library room on a Thursday afternoon at the end of the month, that I had not, until tonight, replayed. *We have, you and I, the same quarrel with this house*, she had said. *We are simply quarrelling about a different room in it.* I had not asked her which room. She had not, in the doorway, lingered long enough to be asked. I have, on the second floor at twenty past eleven on this Wednesday, the time to ask the question. I do not have the person to ask.

Tea tonight: breakfast, gone cold by the time I sat down to write this. I left the chamomile to Hen.

One last thing for the page. Maggie was the last to leave the Library Room this afternoon. She folded her napkin a second time before she stood — the small private gesture she makes when she has agreed to be alone with a question for the night. I should rather Maggie were not alone with the question. I shall, in the morning, suggest a second cup of something to which she has not yet given a name.

Hen made one of her threats again. She doesn't usually mean them. But I don't think Felix knows that.

=== CHAPTER AFTER (Chapter 3, published — full text) ===
## Bookend Chapter C (published text)

# Chapter 3 — Tea and Digestives

The Library Room had not been used for the Society on a Thursday before. Hugo had registered the breach by sitting in a different chair, which is the only kind of objection Hugo allows himself in domestic settings. Dennis had brought a notebook he did not own and a pencil he had borrowed without permission from the porter's desk. Maggie had brought lapsang in her own pot, because the kitchen, this afternoon, was not to be relied upon for anything other than producing the wrong sort of cake at the wrong sort of moment. Iris had brought herself, and her cardigan, and a face that the others recognised as the face she keeps for things she has not yet given herself permission to name.

The porter, of his own accord, had lit the fire at half past two, an hour and a half earlier than he would have lit it on a Wednesday. He had also, of his own accord, brought up the plate of digestives. He had set them down on the long table without comment, and he had — and this was the part Hugo had remarked on, half an hour later, when the porter was safely out of earshot — closed the door behind him on his way out, which the porter had never, in his five years at the Hall, closed without being asked. The Hall was, on this Thursday, treating itself with a small extra care.

Outside the Library Room, beyond the closed door, the building had absorbed the morning's news in the way old buildings do, which is to say imperfectly and with selective acoustics. There were small clusters of residents in the conservatory, none of whom had ordinarily been in the conservatory at this hour on a Thursday. There was a small note pinned to the noticeboard at the foot of the main staircase that said, in Felix Ardent's small careful printing, *We are sorry to announce*, and then the rest, in a sentence that had been written and rewritten three times that morning and that had, in the end, settled into the kind of sentence Felix would have read aloud at a residents' association meeting. There were two cars in the front drive that did not belong to anyone resident. The Hall, in short, knew. The Hall was being kind about it. The Hall was waiting to find out what was going to be done next.

The fire was lit. The plate of digestives sat untouched on the long table, on the principle that no one wished to be the first person on a Thursday afternoon in March to eat a digestive in a room where, one storey above, Henrietta Vance had spent the morning being a problem for the duty doctor. Down the corridor and two doors away from the Library Room was a door behind which DS Khatri had been most of the morning, and beyond it a room with a woman in it who would not be sitting up again.

The afternoon had the not-quite-still quality of a house in which a great deal had happened and very little of it had concluded. Outside, the cedar was still full of starlings, behaving as if they had not noticed. Inside, the four of them sat with their hands in their laps and looked at the fire for slightly longer than is comfortable.

"We have, between us," Hugo began, "approximately one hundred and seventy years of professional experience in looking at facts and deciding what to do with them. I propose we do not waste it."

"You propose," said Dennis, "that we investigate."

"I propose," said Hugo, "that we begin by being honest about the position from which we are looking. The position is this. We are four residents of a house in which an event has occurred. We have no instruction. We have no authority. We have no obligation. We have, however, two of us who saw the deceased in apparent good health on Wednesday afternoon, and one of us" — he did not look at Iris when he said this — "who stood beside the bed this morning before the ambulance arrived. That is not nothing. That is, *in toto*, considerably more than DS Khatri downstairs has by way of useful witnesses, although I would not, of course, tell her so."

"Quietly," Maggie said. "We do this quietly. The police are at the door. The doctor was upstairs an hour ago. The post-mortem will be Friday or Saturday at the latest. If we make ourselves loud, we will make Hen's reputation loud, and that will be no kindness to her or to anyone who ever cared about her."

"Agreed," said Iris.

"Agreed," said Dennis. "And I should rather not have DS Khatri spending her morning telling four respectable elderly people to mind their own business. I should find it humiliating."

"You would find it briefly amusing," said Maggie, "and then humiliating."

"That," said Dennis, "is fair."

Hugo, who had been a barrister long enough to know when a room had already agreed with itself, settled his hands on his knees. "Very well. Quietly. We watch, and we do not run. We do not telephone the press. We do not corner Felix Ardent in his office. We do not ask DS Khatri leading questions; if she chooses to answer one of mine, I shall let her be impressed by herself. Iris — what do we have so far, by way of facts."

The four of them turned to her, and Iris was reminded that *facts* in Hugo's mouth was a verb as well as a noun. She thought about it. She had been thinking about it for six hours.

"We have," she said, "Wednesday afternoon, in this room. A woman who, in our hearing, had written to the bishop's office to ask for an appointment on Friday morning, the purpose of which she described as *two matters connected in their way*. We have a woman who, on the same afternoon, was visibly cross with Felix Ardent over the maintenance accounts, the cross-ness having been ongoing for a month and now, in her words, *not waiting for an audit*."

"Connected in their way," Hugo said. "We do not yet know in which way they are connected."

"No."

"Felix at one end of the bishop's string," said Dennis. "Something else at the other."

"Yes."

"And we have," said Maggie, with a small carefulness that the others recognised, "Wednesday at nine in the evening, a cup of chamomile, on a saucer, on a bedside table. Three quarters drunk. The chamomile prepared, as always, by Effie. Brought in, as always, by Effie. I do not have, as yet, anything more specific to say about it than that. I have rung Dr Walsh, who is duty this morning, and asked him to be very careful with the cup."

She did not look at Iris when she said it. Iris had spent a lifetime noticing when she was not being looked at, and let it pass.

"There is one more thing," Iris said, "which I shall put on the table for completeness. The glasses, on the book, on the bedside table. The wrong way round, by Hen's own habit. Hen folded her glasses and laid them to the right of the book. They were on top of the book this morning."

"How sure are you," said Maggie.

"Sure."

"That is more than a detail, Iris."

"It is one detail. I have only ever asked it to be one detail."

Hugo looked from one to the other. "Noted. We continue. Dennis."

Dennis, who had been waiting for his turn the way a man waits for the second course, sat up. "I'll do what I do. I'll walk into the village this evening. I'll see Bert Pollick, because Bert sees everyone and everyone sees Bert and the village will say what the village is saying inside of an hour. I'll go to the Drovers. I'll go to the post office. I will not, on this occasion, mention the bishop. I will mention Felix, because the village will mention Felix, and it would look stranger not to."

"Bert," said Hugo, "is to be taken half-seriously and not less than half-seriously. He's been right twice that I know of and wrong about a dozen other things, and the trouble is one does not know which kind of week it is for him until it has finished."

"I shall take him a half-pint and a sympathetic ear," said Dennis. "He is owed it."

"I, in turn," said Hugo, "shall look at Eldermarsh Estates on paper. Slowly. In my experience the paper does not lie even when the man holding it does. I will find Felix on a page somewhere. I am not, this week, going to find him in his office."

"Maggie."

"Maggie watches the cup," said Maggie. "And the rest of the medication side of the house. I keep my friends in the surgery; I shall have a small word with one of them about toxicology when one is to be had. I shall not, today, have it. I shall walk past the medication room this evening and notice what is to be noticed. I shall not go in. There is a difference between noticing and rummaging, and our half-promise to Khatri sits on the right side of that difference."

"Iris."

"Iris," said Iris, "writes things down."

"More than that," said Hugo, gently.

"More than that," she conceded. "Iris talks to people who will not, after this morning, want to be talked to. I shall start with Effie, this evening, with the door open and a cup of tea on the table and the pretence of carrying a casserole the cook will let me have if I ask in the right voice."

"Effie."

"Effie."

"And after Effie?"

"After Effie I do not yet know. I should rather not promise the second conversation before I have had the first one."

"That is professional of you, Iris."

"I have been told it is the only kind of professional I am."

Hugo allowed himself one small smile of the kind he kept for the moments at which he was not, in court, on the record. Maggie's mouth moved in what would have been an acknowledgement of the same joke in a different month. Dennis, more openly, laughed once and stopped himself. The room, briefly, had the small civilised quality of a room that had not yet broken in half.

"One more thing," Hugo said. "We have, between us, a finite amount of weekly currency for being seen to be eccentric. We do not have unlimited supply. DS Khatri has been in the building once. She will be in the building twice. We should manage, on the second visit, not to be seen to be doing what we are in fact doing. I am not asking us to hide. I am asking us to be the kind of four people one expects to find in a Library Room at three on a Thursday afternoon."

"That is most of the time who we are."

"It is. We have not, this week, the luxury of being any other kind."

"Good," said Hugo. "Good." He looked round the four of them. "We agree, do we, the four of us, that we tell no one outside this room that we are doing any of it. Not the porter. Not the warden. Not, certainly, the bishop. Not — and I underline the not — DS Khatri."

"Agreed."

"Agreed."

"Agreed," said Iris.

Hugo nodded once, picked up his coat, and left without consulting the digestives. Dennis stood, glanced at Iris, glanced at Maggie, said, *Right then* in the voice he uses when he has nothing further to add, and followed Hugo into the corridor.

The fire shifted. A starling outside made a noise it had no business making in March. Iris waited until the corridor had finished with Hugo's footsteps and Dennis's footsteps and the second set of footsteps that follow Dennis everywhere because Dennis has a knee.

Maggie did not, immediately, speak. She sat with her hands folded on her lap, the lapsang gone cold beside her, the fire shifting itself smaller. There had been, in her surgery at Brixton between 1992 and 2018, eight deaths Maggie had not been able to write a confident certificate for, in the standard meaning of *confident*; she had told Iris, the previous autumn, the number, and had not, then or since, told her the names. *I keep them in a list*, she had said, *because nobody else will, and I will not, on the back of one of them, refuse to ask the question again.* Maggie, this Thursday, was asking the question again. Iris could see her asking. There was no other reading of the way Maggie was sitting.

The afternoon got darker outside the cedar. The starling moved on. Neither of them suggested another tea.

Iris had not yet asked her question. She had been carrying it for six hours.

"Maggie."

"Iris."

"Could it have been the tea?"

Maggie looked at her over the cup of lapsang. She had had the lapsang in her hand throughout. She had let it go cold without drinking it because she had been waiting for this question.

"Could be a lot of things," Maggie said. "Most of them you'd have to know your way around a medicine cabinet to manage."

tasks.groundTruth:
# Chapter 2 — The Body

::: dateline
Thursday — 8:14am.
:::

Maggie knocked at six minutes past eight. I know the hour because I had just put the kettle on and the kettle is loud and the knock had to come twice. *Come quickly*, she said, when I opened. *Effie is with her, but I shouldn't have Effie there alone.* She did not say *with her*. She did not have to. The way Maggie says the word *her* in the morning, before anything has been done about it, is enough.

Maggie was in her dressing gown. Maggie was, for the first time in eleven months, in her dressing gown outside her flat. She had her stethoscope round her neck because she had grabbed it before she had grabbed her cardigan; she had her glasses on; she had not, in the kitchen, finished her own tea. The kettle in her flat was, even from the corridor, still going. She had heard a sound from the staircase — Effie's, she said later — and had come down the stairs in her slippers and had looked at the open door and at Effie sitting in the corridor and had known, in the half-second she had spent looking, what kind of corridor it was. She had gone in. She had come straight back out to find me.

I took the kettle off. I did not look for shoes. I went in the slippers I have been wearing since 2019, because they have the seam along the outside, and the seam along the outside still matters in a way I do not propose to explain in this notebook.

The corridor ran longer than usual, the way it does when you do not want to arrive. Effie was sitting on the floor outside Hen's door with her back against the panelling and her hand over her mouth. She is twenty-six. I have always tried to remember that.

"She wasn't there," Effie said. "I said her name. I said her name twice."

"I know," said Maggie. "Iris is going to sit with you a moment."

"I came in with the breakfast. The eight-o'clock. I always knock first. I knocked first. She didn't answer and I — I came in. I thought she had slept through. She did it once before, when the lavender man came for the cushions and she pretended for an hour to be deaf."

"She had slept through," Maggie said, in the voice she keeps for the things she has decided people are allowed to believe. "She has just done it the long way."

Effie laughed for less than a second and was sorry for it before the sound had quite stopped.

I went in.

Hen was in her bed and Hen looked, as far as I could allow myself to look, like Hen asleep. The duvet was up to her shoulders. Her hands were on the coverlet, the right over the left, the way she liked. Her mouth was closed. The room held the small clean shape of a room where nothing has happened. The lamp on the bedside table was off. The curtains were drawn but not pulled tight; one inch of grey morning came in down the middle. The cup on the bedside table was three-quarters drunk. A digestive sat untouched on the saucer.

The room belongs, properly, to a woman who had been in a hurry, six years ago, to leave a London flat that had become too big and had brought with her only what she had decided she would still need, which had turned out to be more than she had told herself but less than she could have brought. Two framed prints on the wall — one a Stanley Spencer reproduction Hen had not, since 2019, hung straight. The bookcase across from the bed, three shelves, alphabetical by author, paperback the lower two and hardcover the top. The writing-bureau by the window with the small key on its own ribbon in the lock, because Hen had decided that pretending to lock the bureau was sufficient. There were no photographs in the room, which had always struck me as a thing about Hen worth noting and which struck me again this morning, in a way I did not, in front of Maggie, examine.

I let myself look at the cup. The cup was the everyday white, not the blue. The handle was at four o'clock. The saucer was set parallel to the edge of the table, which is to say the way Effie sets it down — not the way Hen would have set it down if she had moved it after.

I let myself look at the book.

The book on the bedside table was the one Hen had been reading at the meeting on Wednesday — Mantel, the third volume, with its grey paperback spine. The book was lying flat. On top of the book — on top of, not next to, not inside, not propping it, not weighing down its pages — sat Hen's reading glasses, neatly folded, the left arm tucked over the right.

I had not, until that moment, registered that I had a small alphabet for Hen's habits. Hen folded her glasses and laid them on the table to the right of the book. Hen marked her page with the back of the dust jacket. Hen did not put her glasses on top of her book, ever, because she had once told me, at length, that a book closed under a pair of glasses was an insult to both objects. *I am not yet so blind*, she had said, *that I cannot find my book without first lifting my eyes off it.*

The glasses were on the book.

Behind the book, against the base of the bedside lamp, was a corner of paper. Not the whole sheet — a quarter of one, torn along two edges, the kind of corner one finds when a notepad has given up half a page. Two part-words half-visible, the rest run off the edge: *T—* on the first line, *fr—* on the second. The handwriting was not Hen's. The handwriting was a younger hand and an upright one, not quite ballpoint — a fountain pen kept loose at the nib, which is a thing a particular sort of person does.

I did not look at Maggie. Maggie was looking at Hen's wrist, with her own wristwatch turned up, doing what Maggie does. I stepped closer to the bedside table on the pretext of straightening the cup, and I put the corner of paper in the pocket of my cardigan as though it had always lived there.

I did not know why. I knew it was the wrong thing for the police to find and not be able to ask Hen about. That is a sentence that would not have left my mouth a year ago. It is what came out of my hand.

The bedside table itself is a small mahogany piece with one drawer that does not, this morning, look as if it had been opened. The lamp's pull-cord hung straight down — Hen's habit was to leave it tucked against the lamp's neck so it did not, at six in the morning, brush her cheek when she turned to look at the clock. The clock — a small travel-clock from a hotel in Vienna in 1981 — read four minutes fast as it always read four minutes fast. The book of matches she kept by the candle she did not, in the end, ever burn was where it had been on Tuesday. Nothing else, that I could see, was where it had not been on Tuesday, except for the glasses.

"I have called them," Maggie said. "They will be here in fifteen. Perhaps less; she is the duty doctor's reason to set out today. Heart, almost certainly. I shall make the case for heart. I shall not, in this house, make the other case until I have a reason."

"Is there a reason?"

Maggie did not look at me. "There is a cup of tea three-quarters drunk on the bedside table. The doctor will look at the cup. So shall I. I am not yet looking at it." She turned her watch back the right way on her wrist. "Take Effie down to the Sun Room. Bert is already in there with his crossword. He will not say anything that helps. He will also not say anything that wounds. He has his uses. Don't let Effie go back to the cottage on her own."

"She will want to go."

"I am aware of what she will want. Take her to the Sun Room."

I took Effie down to the Sun Room. She walked with her arms folded across her chest as if she had been outside in February. At the bottom of the stairs she stopped and said, *I never know how to do this part*, and I said *I do not think any of us know*, and she let me put my hand on her elbow, which is not a thing she would have allowed me yesterday. Bert was in the Sun Room with his crossword and a cold mug of something, and he looked up at us once and went back to his crossword without comment, which was, in its way, kind. He had been reading the *Telegraph* of two days previous and he had not closed it to indicate that he had noticed our arrival, which was, also in its way, kind. He had been at Larkspur Hall longer than any of us and he had the gift, among the few he had publicly retained, of knowing when a young woman in distress would rather not be looked at.

The ambulance arrived at twenty-three minutes past eight. The duty doctor was in Hen's room for forty-one minutes. A police constable arrived at the quarter-hour. Then a second constable. Then, at twenty past ten, a woman in a dark coat who introduced herself, in the corridor, as DS Khatri, and who did not look pleased to be in our hall on a Thursday morning. I noted her, and walked on. She had on a coat that was darker than her work required; she had on shoes that were sensible without quite committing to it; she had a notebook in her hand of the sort that one buys oneself rather than the sort that the office provides. I noted these things in the corridor. I did not, at the time, know I was noting them in the way that I would later remember having noted them.

She asked me, on her way past, whether I was *the lady who had been first in the room with Dr Okoye*, and I told her that I was. She asked, then, whether I would mind being on the list of people her constables would be speaking to in the afternoon. I told her I would not mind. She thanked me. She did not, on the way down the corridor, make any further conversation. Behind her, in the open doorway of Hen's flat, the duty doctor was talking to the younger of the two constables. The younger constable was writing things down. I did not, on my way past, slow.

::: dateline
Thursday — later.
:::

I have closed my door. I have put the kettle on a second time, and this time it has been allowed to come to the boil. Tea: breakfast. I cannot face anything more complicated than breakfast.

In my own flat I took the corner of paper out of my pocket. *T—* and *fr—*. I laid it on the desk under the lamp. I looked at it for a long time without making it tell me anything. *T—* might be *to* and *fr—* might be *from*. Or *T—* might be a name beginning with a capital. Or it might be the corner of a word, *not* a name — *touched* — *trouble* — *teacher*. Cryptic clues do not like to be the first solved.

I put the corner of paper inside the back cover of this notebook and I have not, this afternoon, taken it out again.

Two things I shall add, for the record. Maggie did not tell Khatri about the glasses, which means Maggie is waiting; Maggie waiting is a different thing from Maggie not knowing. The cup of chamomile was three quarters drunk and Hen did not, as a rule, leave a quarter in the bottom of a cup unless the maker had done something to it. Hen finished her tea. Hen finished her tea even when she was angry with it. There was a quarter in the cup. I shall not, this afternoon, decide what that means; I shall only put it on the page.

Effie has gone back to the cottage with the porter's wife and the porter's wife will sit with her for the afternoon. Maggie has gone, I believe, to do what doctors do in the hour after, which she has not, in my hearing, ever called by a name. Hugo has rung once already. Dennis has rung twice. I have agreed, the four of us, to meet at three. The Library Room. The fire if the porter has remembered. We shall not, this Thursday, be ordinary about it.

I am writing this so that I shall remember to be honest about what I did. I straightened a cup I had no need to straighten. I took a piece of paper that was not mine. I did not ring the bell when I went into the room and the bell was right there. I pocketed it before I had decided anything. That is the line in this entry I am going to come back to.

criteria.name: continuity_with_bookends
criteria.guidance: Does the chapter honor what Chapter 1 planted (the 9pm chamomile ritual, Hen's threat against Felix, Effie bringing tea, the diarist narrator) and set up cleanly what Chapter 3 then acts on (the body, the wrongness of the glasses on the book, the tea cup, the Society deciding to investigate)? Penalise any contradiction of the bookends, dropped thread, or continuity gap HARD.

criteria.name: beat_fidelity
criteria.guidance: Does it land the required middle-chapter beats from the story context's beat-sheet — Hen's death discovered, Iris's first-scene forensic observations, the wrongness that flags murder — without inventing events that break the fair-play mystery?

criteria.name: scene_craft
criteria.guidance: Concrete, in-scene, show-don't-tell; specific sensory detail; no summary narration or throat-clearing.

criteria.name: prose_voice
criteria.guidance: Matches the established cozy voice, the locked-diarist first-person POV and tense, dry warmth; varied sentence rhythm; no clichés or AI tells.
